agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services. The CEO described it at an all-hands meeting with high-level vision but minimal specifics. The system adjusts delivery prices based on demand levels, aiming to balance competitiveness with profitability, and will operate in a fully automated manner with some form of price guardrails.","bounded_context":"Pricing / Rate Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system"},{"term":"Demand","definition":"A measure of current delivery request volume relative to capacity — the primary input signal for price adjustments. Exact measurement method is unspecified."},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds or constraints on automated price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing outcomes"},{"term":"Real-time pricing","definition":"The ability to adjust delivery prices dynamically as conditions change, rather than using static rate cards"},{"term":"Fully automated","definition":"The system adjusts prices without requiring manual human intervention for each pricing decision"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to a baseline or capacity threshold","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The direction of price movement relative to demand is a core business policy, but 'high demand' is undefined — the threshold, magnitude, and curve of increase are all policy decisions a VP could change","testable_assertion":"Given demand exceeds the configured high-demand threshold, when a delivery price is calculated, then the price must be greater than the base price"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to a baseline or capacity threshold","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Symmetric to BR-1 — a business policy decision about competitive positioning during low demand. The threshold and magnitude are tunable.","testable_assertion":"Given demand is below the configured low-demand threshold, when a delivery price is calculated, then the price must be less than the base price"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Price adjustments must occur in real time — prices reflect current demand conditions, not stale or batched data","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"'Real time' is a latency policy. The acceptable staleness window (sub-second? minutes?) is a business decision, not a physical constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given demand conditions change, when a delivery price is requested, then the price reflects demand data no older than the configured freshness threshold"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The pricing system must operate in a fully automated manner without requiring manual human approval for individual price changes","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Automation level is a business policy. The company could decide to add manual approval for certain scenarios (e.g., surge above a threshold) without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given the system is operational, when demand conditions change, then prices are recalculated and applied without manual intervention"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Price guardrails must exist to constrain the range of automated price adjustments, preventing prices from reaching extreme values","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without guardrails, the system could produce prices that are zero/negative (destroying revenue) or astronomically high (destroying trust and potentially violating consumer protection laws). The existence of guardrails is a system integrity constraint — though the specific values are policy.","testable_assertion":"Given any demand level (including extreme spikes or zero demand), when a price is calculated, then the resulting price must fall within the configured minimum and maximum price bounds"},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A calculated delivery price must always be a positive monetary value","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A zero or negative delivery price is nonsensical — it would mean paying customers to receive deliveries. This is a fundamental data integrity constraint regardless of demand levels.","testable_assertion":"Given any demand condition, when a delivery price is calculated, then the price is strictly greater than zero"},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The pricing function must be monotonically related to demand — higher demand must never produce a lower price than lower demand, all else being equal","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If higher demand produced lower prices, the system would contradict its core economic purpose and create perverse incentives (e.g., arbitrage). This is a logical consistency constraint on the pricing function itself.","testable_assertion":"Given two demand levels D1 > D2 with all other inputs equal, when prices are calculated for each, then Price(D1) >= Price(D2)"}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"Demand is above the high-demand threshold","when":"A delivery price is calculated","then":"The price is higher than the base/standard delivery price"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"Demand is below the low-demand threshold","when":"A delivery price is calculated","then":"The price is lower than the base/standard delivery price"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Demand spikes to an extreme level","when":"A delivery price is calculated","then":"The price does not exceed the configured maximum price guardrail"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"Demand drops to zero or near-zero","when":"A delivery price is calculated","then":"The price does not fall below the configured minimum price guardrail and remains positive"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"Demand conditions change","when":"A new delivery price request arrives","then":"The price reflects current demand data within the configured freshness SLA"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"The system is running normally","when":"Demand fluctuates throughout the day","then":"Prices adjust automatically without any manual intervention or approval step"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand data feed becomes unavailable or stale","expected_behavior":"Undefined — should the system fall back to base prices, use last-known demand, or halt pricing? This is a critical gap."},{"scenario":"Demand oscillates rapidly around a threshold boundary","expected_behavior":"Undefined — prices could flicker between high and low states, creating a poor customer experience. Dampening/smoothing policy is unspecified."},{"scenario":"Multiple simultaneous orders during a demand spike","expected_behavior":"Undefined — should all orders in the same time window get the same price, or can prices change between individual requests within the same spike?"},{"scenario":"Demand is exactly at baseline — neither high nor low","expected_behavior":"Undefined — the CEO only described high and low demand scenarios. Presumably base price applies, but no neutral band is defined."},{"scenario":"Geographic variation in demand","expected_behavior":"Undefined — is demand measured globally, per city, per zone? A spike in one area shouldn't necessarily affect pricing in another."},{"scenario":"Guardrail values themselves need to change","expected_behavior":"Undefined — who has authority to change guardrails, what approval process is needed, and should changes take effect immediately or be staged?"},{"scenario":"Customer sees one price, but demand changes before order confirmation","expected_behavior":"Undefined — price locking/quoting policy during the order flow is not addressed. This is a major UX and trust concern."},{"scenario":"Predatory pricing or anti-competitive concerns","expected_behavior":"Undefined — in some jurisdictions, automated surge pricing or predatory low pricing may violate consumer protection or competition laws. No legal/regulatory framework mentioned."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Dynamic pricing directly impacts revenue and margins. Correct implementation could significantly increase revenue during peak demand while maintaining volume during off-peak. Incorrect guardrails could either leave money on the table (too tight) or destroy customer trust (too loose).","operational":"Full automation reduces manual pricing overhead but introduces operational risk — monitoring, alerting, and override capabilities are critical. Demand measurement infrastructure must be reliable and low-latency.","user":"Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price volatility may drive customers to competitors. Conversely, lower off-peak prices could increase adoption.","risk":"High risk due to vagueness of specification. Regulatory exposure (surge pricing laws, price gouging statutes), reputational risk (perception of exploiting customers during high demand), and technical risk (stale data, race conditions in pricing). The CEO's 'probably' regarding guardrails is alarming — this should be a hard requirement.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — will pay variable prices. Trust and satisfaction depend on transparency, fairness perception, and guardrail effectiveness."},{"group":"Delivery couriers/drivers","impact":"Indirectly affected — demand-based pricing may shift delivery volumes across time windows, affecting earnings and workload distribution."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue team","impact":"Must understand and forecast revenue under dynamic pricing. Need visibility into pricing decisions for reporting and auditing."},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Demand signals used for pricing may also be relevant for capacity planning and courier allocation."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Must review pricing model against consumer protection regulations, anti-gouging laws, and contractual obligations."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will receive complaints about price variability. Need tooling to explain why a specific price was charged."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"The CEO's description is an elevator pitch, not a specification. Almost every critical parameter is undefined: demand measurement methodology, threshold definitions, adjustment magnitudes, guardrail values, geographic scope, temporal granularity, price locking policy, fallback behavior, and regulatory constraints. The word 'probably' before guardrails is particularly concerning for a system that directly determines what customers pay. There are enough signals to extract directional business rules, but nowhere near enough to build a correct system.","high_confidence_areas":["Prices go up when demand is high (directional rule is clear)","Prices go down when demand is low (directional rule is clear)","System should be automated","Some form of guardrails will exist"],"low_confidence_areas":["How demand is measured (volume? capacity ratio? what baseline?)","What constitutes 'high' vs 'low' demand (thresholds undefined)","Magnitude and curve of price adjustments (linear? exponential? tiered?)","Specific guardrail values (min/max prices)","Geographic granularity of demand measurement","Temporal granularity ('real time' is vague — seconds? minutes?)","Price locking/quoting policy during order flow","Fallback behavior when demand data is unavailable","Whether 'competitive' implies competitor price awareness or just lower off-peak prices","Regulatory and legal constraints on dynamic pricing","Whether guardrails are truly mandatory (CEO said 'probably')"],"assumptions":["Guardrails ARE mandatory despite the CEO's 'probably' — a pricing system without bounds is a business risk that no responsible implementation should accept","A base/standard price exists that serves as the anchor for adjustments","Demand is measured relative to some capacity or historical baseline","The pricing function is monotonic with respect to demand","Price must always be a positive monetary value"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The CEO said 'probably' regarding guardrails. This MUST be resolved — are guardrails a hard requirement or optional? For a system that autonomously sets customer-facing prices, the absence of guardrails is a critical business and legal risk.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Legal/Compliance","Finance/Revenue team"],"options":["Guardrails are mandatory with defined min/max bounds","Guardrails are optional and can be toggled on/off","Guardrails are mandatory but values are self-adjusting"],"recommendation":"Guardrails must be mandatory. Treat this as a non-negotiable system invariant regardless of the CEO's hedging."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Demand' is referenced as the primary pricing input but is never defined. Demand could mean: absolute order volume, order volume relative to courier capacity, order volume relative to historical average, or order volume relative to a time-window forecast. The choice fundamentally changes system behavior.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations/Dispatch","Finance/Revenue team","Customers"],"options":["Absolute order count per time window","Ratio of orders to available courier capacity","Deviation from historical demand baseline","Composite signal combining multiple factors"],"recommendation":"Demand should be measured as a ratio of current order volume to available courier/delivery capacity, as this captures both sides of the supply-demand equation."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Real time' is undefined. This could mean sub-second pricing updates, per-minute recalculation, or simply 'not a static rate card updated monthly.' The engineering cost and architecture differ dramatically across interpretations.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations/Dispatch","Customers","Engineering"],"options":["Sub-second (event-driven, per-request calculation)","Per-minute batch recalculation","Per-15-minute window updates","On-demand recalculation when demand crosses a threshold"],"recommendation":"Clarify acceptable latency. For delivery pricing, per-minute recalculation is likely sufficient and dramatically simpler than sub-second."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Competitive but also profitable' presents a tension without resolution. No pricing floor tied to cost structure is mentioned, no competitor awareness mechanism is described, and no target margin is stated. These are essential inputs to a pricing algorithm.","affected_stakeholders":["Finance/Revenue team","Customers","Executive leadership"],"options":["Define minimum margin percentage as a hard floor","Use competitor price feeds as an input signal","Set competitiveness targets per market segment","Let guardrail minimums implicitly define the profitability floor"],"recommendation":"Define a cost-based price floor (ensuring profitability) separate from the demand-based guardrail minimum. Competitiveness should be addressed through market analysis, not embedded in the algorithm without clear parameters."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
